segments.s410-g57: پشوتن is the proper name (subject). بدو is the dative clitic + prepositional phrase 'to him' (short for به او). گفت is a past-tense verb 'said' placed after subject and indirect object — common in Persian narrative word order. اینست راه is an inverted copular clause meaning 'this is the way' (اینست = این است, a poetic/contracted form).
segments.s410-g58: بر این باش is an imperative instruction: بر + این ('upon/with this') + باش (imperative 'be/stand') meaning 'adhere to this' or 'observe this'. و links two imperatives. آزرم is a poetic/obscure noun 'honor/esteem' and مردان is the genitive 'of men' (the possessors of that honor). بخواه is imperative (2nd person singular) from خواستن meaning 'seek/wish for' (here: 'seek the honor'). The subject 'تو' is implied in both imperatives.
segments.s410-g1: This is a temporal phrase: به + شبگیر + هنگام + بانگِ خروس = 'at the night-time/at the hour of the cock's cry.' به functions as a temporal preposition; شبگیر is an adjective/noun meaning 'night-time' or 'nocturnal'; هنگام introduces 'the time of'; بانگِ خروس is a genitive (بانگ +ِ + خروس) meaning 'the cry/crowing of the rooster.' Word order is typical of classical Persian temporal phrases where modifiers precede هنگام.
segments.s410-g2: ز درگاه (adverbial) + برخاست (past verb) + آوای کوس (subject in construct) = 'from the portal rose the sound of the drum.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; برخاست is a past tense verb 'rose/was raised'; آوای کوس is a possessive/construct phrase ('the sound of the drum') functioning as the noun that 'rose.' The verb precedes its subject here, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s410-g3: چو introduces a simile/conjunction 'as' or 'like.' پیلی is used adjectivally/simile-wise 'elephantine, like an elephant.' به اسپ = 'onto the horse' with به as a directional preposition. اندر آورد پای is a verbal phrase 'put/placed (his) foot in/onto' where اندر is an older prepositional element 'in/into' and آورد (from آوردن) means 'brought/put' — here 'set/placed.' The object پای follows the verb due to poetic inversion.
segments.s410-g4: بیاورد is here causative/past-like 'caused/made (to move)' or simply 'moved' in the narrative sense; چون باد is another simile 'like the wind' (چون = 'like'), and لشکر ز جای = 'the army from its place(s),' i.e. 'the army sprang from their positions.' The clause describes the immediate, wind-like movement of the host; word order places the verb before its subject/phrase for emphasis.
segments.s410-g5: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle meaning 'kept on/was still,' and رفت is the past 'went.' تا marks the limit 'until,' and پیشِش آمد literally 'came before him' = 'he reached/encountered.' دو راه is the noun phrase 'two paths' (a fork). Note the verb آمد precedes the noun 'دو راه' — a common inversion in classical style.
segments.s410-g6: فرو ماند is a compound where فرو (down/intensifier) combines with ماند (past of ماندن 'remain') to mean 'remained/stood fast/was left behind.' بر جای = 'on the spot/in place,' and پیل و سپاه is the coordinated subject 'the elephant and the army.' The verb comes before its subject here, so 'they remained in place.'
segments.s410-g7: دژ گنبدان is a genitive/place-name phrase 'the fortress of Gonbadān' (or 'the domed fortress'); بود is the past copula 'was/there was'; راهش is 'its road' (راه + possessive -ش) and یکی = 'one.' The clause means 'the fortress had one road' or 'its road was one,' with classical word order putting the predicate after بود.
segments.s410-g8: دگر سوی = 'the other side/different direction' (دگر + سوی), زااول 'from the first/from the outset' (ز + اول), کشید is the past of کشیدن 'drew/ran/extended' meaning 'ran/led toward,' and اندکی 'a little' modifies the motion (i.e. 'veered/bent slightly'). Together: 'another way, from the start, ran a little toward the other side.'
segments.s410-g25: وزانجا is a fused adverbial (= 'and/from there') placing the action's origin; بیامد is a 3rd-person singular past tense verb 'he came/went'; سوی هیرمند is a directional phrase ('toward Helmand') with the preposition سوی before the place. The overall order is adverbial – verb – directional object, typical of epic Persian.
segments.s410-g26: همی is a continuative adverb here meaning 'still' or 'continually'; بود is the past copula 'was' (3rd sing.), and ترسان is a predicative adjective 'afraid'. The phrase ز بیم گزند ('from/because of fear (بیم) of harm (گزند)') gives the cause. Together they mean 'he was still afraid because of fear of harm.'
segments.s410-g27: بر آیین is an adverbial phrase meaning 'according to custom' (lit. 'on custom'); ببستند is 3rd-person plural past 'they closed/tied/fastened'; پرده‌سرای is the object 'the curtain-hall/pavilion' placed after the verb. The segment states a customary action performed by the group.
segments.s410-g28: بزرگان لشگر is a nominal group 'the great ones/elders of the army' (noun + genitive-like modifier). گزیدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they chose', and جای is the direct object 'place' (i.e. they selected a site). Word order here is Subject (leaders) – Verb – Object.
segments.s410-g29: شراعی is an agent noun (here an official/ceremonial officer); بزد is the 3rd-singular past 'he struck' and is followed by the adverb زود 'quickly/soon' (adverb after verb is common in poetry). و links to the next clause; بنهاد is another 3rd-singular past 'he placed/established', and تخت is its object 'throne'. The sequence describes two quick ceremonial actions.
segments.s410-g30: بر آن تخت is a locative/prepositional phrase 'on that throne'. بر شد here functions as a compound meaning 'mounted/ascended' (preposition + شد, with شد as past 'became/was'), so بر شد = 'rose onto' the throne. گو introduces an epithet and links to نیک‌بخت: 'گو نیک‌بخت' = 'the one called/being the fortunate one.'
segments.s410-g31: می‌آورد is the imperfective/formal present-stem often used in narrative with progressive or habitual sense 'he brought/was bringing'; و links the actions. رامشگران را uses را as the definite object marker 'the peacemakers' and بخواند is a 3rd-singular past (here 'he called/summoned' — sense 'to call/summon'). The clause means he brought and then summoned those peacemakers.
segments.s410-g32: بسی is an intensifier 'much/many' modifying the following nouns; زر و گوهر are the items 'gold and jewels'. بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'upon them' and فشاند is the 3rd-singular past 'he scattered/sprinkled'. The verb governs the prepositional object: 'he scattered much gold and jewels upon them.'
segments.s410-g9: شتر (subject) + انک (archaic exclamatory 'behold/now') + در پیش (prepositional phrase 'in front/ahead') + بودش (past copula with enclitic '-ش' = 'it was') + بخفت (past of 'خفت' 'to sleep'). Word order is poetic: verb phrase 'بودش بخفت' follows the locative 'در پیش'.
segments.s410-g10: تو گفتی is an unexpressed citation ('you said') introducing a reported clause; که introduces that-clause. گشتست is an archaic perfect/resultative ('has become' — the -ست is an old form like است). با marks accompaniment/union and خاک جفت is a figurative idiom 'paired with the dust' = reduced to dust/dead.
segments.s410-g11: همی is a classical verbal particle that frames the action (here a momentary/continuous past sense); چوب زد is the verb phrase 'struck with a stick'; بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'upon his head' with the enclitic ش = 'his'; ساروان (the subject) appears after the verb because of poetic inversion.
segments.s410-g12: ز here means 'because of' (classical poetic preposition), رفتن is the verbal noun 'the going/departure', بماند is 'remained' (third person), and آن زمان کاروان ('the caravan at that time') is the delayed subject — literal: 'because of the departure the caravan remained then.'
segments.s410-g13: جهان‌جوی را places 'the traveller' as the person affected (را marks definite object/role here: 'to the traveller'); آن بد آمد به فال is a fixed phrase 'that came as an ill/bad omen' (بد آمد به فال = was an ill omen).
segments.s410-g14: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/said'; what follows is a commanded clause: کش (a verb-form meaning 'pull/strike/force' here giving force to the order) + سر ببرند uses subjunctive/plural ببرند as the reported command 'that they cut off the head'; و یال adds 'and the mane' — poetic ellipsis yields the full command to behead and take the mane.
segments.s410-g15: بدان is an imperative/introductory 'know this' (classical usage). تا introduces a subordinate/temporal/purposive clause 'that/so that'; بدو 'to him' (dative), بازگردد is a present-subjunctive/old perfective-form 'should/shall return', and بدی ('harm/evil') is the noun object that will be negated in the following segment.
segments.s410-g16: نباشد is a negative subjunctive/form meaning 'there be not' or 'shall not be' (here 'there will be no ...'); به جز = 'except', and فره ایزدی is the collocation 'divine farr/glory' (the only thing excepted). Together: 'there will be no harm except the divine farr.'
segments.s410-g17: بریدند is a past plural verb 'they cut' placed before its agent (a common poetic/inverted order). پرخاشجویان is the agent 'the aggressors/assailants' (plural). سرش is the direct object 'his head' — سر 'head' with the possessive enclitic -ش 'his'.
segments.s410-g18: بدو بازگشت is verb phrase 'returned to him' with بدو ('to him') preceding the verb بازگشت ('returned'), a normal Persian order for a verb plus dative pronoun. آن زمان is the adverbial time 'at that time'; اخترش is 'his star' (اختر 'star' + possessive -ش). The whole clause therefore indicates the time when his star 'returned to him'.
segments.s410-g19: غمی گشت is an existential/experiential pattern 'a grief came to be' or 'grief arose' (غمی = 'a grief', گشت = 'became/arose'). زان is the prepositional phrase 'from that' (poetic zā(n) = 'from that'), and اشتر اسفندیار is a proper-name phrase (here treated as 'Ashter Esfandiyar' or a named referent connected to Esfandiyar) that is the source/cause implied by زان.
segments.s410-g20: گرفت here is a past transitive 'seized/took' used of an afflicting force that befalls someone. آن زمان is 'at that time' (time adverbial). اختر شوم خوار is a nominal phrase describing the fate that acted: اختر 'star' (fate/fortune) qualified by the adjectives شوم 'ill‑omened' and خوار 'despised/humbled'. Grammatically, those adjectives follow the noun in this poetic order and together form the agent that 'seized' him.
segments.s410-g21: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus it was said' / 'thus he said' introducing reported speech. کآن کس که پیروز گشت is a relative clause 'that/which person who became victorious' — کآن is an archaic fused form functioning like 'که آن' ('that/which'), کس 'person', که introduces the relative clause, پیروز 'victorious' and گشت the past 'became'.
segments.s410-g22: سر بخت او is a compound where سر 'head/top' modifies بخت 'fortune' (i.e. 'the peak/top of his fortune'); او is the possessive 'his'. گیتی افروز is a compound adjective 'world‑illuminating' (گیتی 'world' + افروز 'illuminating'), and گشت is the past 'became' — together: 'the summit of his fortune became (world‑)illuminating.'
segments.s410-g23: بد و نیک 'bad and good' are a coordinated pair; هر دو 'both (of them)' emphasizes they are both included. ز یزدان 'from God' uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from' and یزدان = 'God/divine power'. بود is the past copula 'were' — together: 'both bad and good were from God.'
segments.s410-g24: لب مرد باید is a short predicative phrase 'a man's lip/mouth ought (must)' where باید is the deontic modal 'must/ought'. که introduces the clause complement and خندان بود uses خندان 'smiling' with بود (here the past copula used in an older/poetic idiom) to express the desired state: 'that a man should be smiling' or more idiomatically 'a man ought to wear a smiling face.'
segments.s410-g33: به here works as an instrumental/benefactive preposition ('with' / 'by'), followed by رامش 'tranquility'. دل خویشتن is the direct object phrase 'his own heart' with خویشتن adding emphasis/possession ('his own'). شاد کرد is a two-part predicate: شاد (an adjective 'happy/glad') plus کرد (past tense 'made') — literally 'made (it) glad'.
segments.s410-g34: دل راد مردان is a noun phrase where راد qualifies دل (literally 'rād-hearted/daring heart') and مردان is the plural 'men' (so: 'the men of brave hearts' / 'the brave men's hearts'). پر از is a fixed pattern meaning 'full of', with از introducing the content (یاد 'memory/remembrance'). کرد is the past tense verb 'made' applying to the whole phrase ('made (them) full of remembrance').
segments.s410-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گل is the subject 'the flower'. بشکفید is a poetic/past-form of 'to bloom' (here 'bloomed'). از here introduces cause or origin ('from' / 'because of') and می‌سالخورد is an archaic/poetic expression meaning 'having long years / grown old' (literally 'has eaten years'), functioning as a temporal qualifier ('because of / with the passing years').
segments.s410-g36: رخ is 'face/appearance' and here heads a noun phrase 'رخِ نامداران' = 'the faces of the renowned'. و joins that phrase to شاه 'the king'. نبرد in classical use can be a noun 'battle' (here) and the phrase as a whole reads as a nominal cluster (‘the faces of the famed and the king — battle’), i.e. the line pictures the famed and the king in relation to battle; the exact clause is elliptical/poetic rather than a simple finitive sentence.
segments.s410-g37: به یاران is a prepositional phrase 'to the companions' (recipient). چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت 'said' in past). کز is the archaic contracted form of 'که + از' here functioning as a subordinating conjunction 'that/from which' introducing the following content, and رای شاه is 'the king's counsel/decision' (رای + possessive شاه).
segments.s410-g38: نپیچیدم is past tense with the negative prefix ن- plus پیچیدم (پیچیدم 'I turned/turned aside') — 'I did not turn aside / did not deviate'. و is simple conjunction 'and'. دور گشتم is a compound of دور ('far/away') + گشتم (past of گشتن, here 'I became/turned/ I went'), meaning 'I went away / I turned away'. ز (poetic form of از) marks the source 'from' and راه is 'the road/path' — together 'I turned away from the road'.
segments.s410-g39: مرا (here written as م + را in the token list) is the accusative 'me' (he told me). گفت is past 'said/told'. بر کار رستم is a prepositional phrase 'regarding/beside the affair of Rostam' (بر used as 'concerning' + کار 'affair' + رستم). بسیچ (بسیج / بسیچ here) is an intensifier 'very/much' indicating he spoke at length about that matter.
segments.s410-g40: ز بند و ز خواری is a paired prepositional phrase 'from bondage and from humiliation' (the poetic preposition ز = از introduces the sources). میاسای is an imperative or jussive form meaning 'be at rest / cease' (here used as a command admonishing not to be at rest). هیچ follows to add negation/intensification 'at all / in no way' — together the phrase reads as a directive: 'do not rest/cease at all from chains and humiliation' (i.e. do not let captivity or dishonor make you complacent).
segments.s410-g41: "به کردن برفتم برای پدر" is a purpose + motion clause: به + infinitive (کردن) expresses purpose 'to do', and برفتم is a poetic/past first‑person motion verb ('I went/set out') built on رفتن with the prefix بر-; برای پدر marks the beneficiary 'for my father'. Together: 'I went to do (it) for my father.'
segments.s410-g42: کنون is an adverb 'now' that frames what follows; این is the demonstrative 'this' modifying the following noun phrase; گزین here functions as an adjective/participle 'chosen/selected' (poetic use of a verbal root as modifier); پیر = 'old, elderly'; پرخاشخر is a descriptive adjective 'quarrelsome/irritable' (poetic coinage). Word order is typical Persian: temporal adverb -> demonstrative -> modifiers -> head (omitted noun 'one' or 'man').
segments.s410-g43: بسی رنج دارد = 'he has/experiences great suffering': بسی is an intensifier 'much/very', رنج 'suffering', and دارد is the 3rd‑person present of داشتن used to mean 'he endures' or 'he has'. به جای سران is a prepositional phrase 'in the stead/room of the chiefs/leaders' (به جای = 'in place of' + سران 'leaders').
segments.s410-g44: جهان راست کرده is a resultative/perfective clause: جهان 'the world' is the object, راست 'right' (adjective) + کرده (perfective of کردن) yields 'has made right' or 'set right'. به گرز گران uses به instrumentally 'with/by' (here 'by means of') + گرز 'mace/club' + گران 'heavy', giving 'with a heavy mace.'
segments.s410-g45: همه شهر ایران is a noun phrase 'all the cities/towns of Iran' (همه modifies شهر). بدو is an indirect/prepositional pronoun 'by him / because of him' and functions as the beneficiary/agent of the following predicate. زنده‌اند is the 3rd‑person plural present copula + adjective 'they are alive / they live.' The clause literally: 'All Iran's towns live by him.'
segments.s410-g46: اگر ... و‌گر ... is a paired conditional meaning 'whether ... or ...': اگر شهریارند = 'if/when they are rulers' and و گر بنده‌اند = 'or if they are servants/subjects.' Both predicates use the 3rd‑person plural copula ending -ند.
segments.s410-g47: فرستاده باید = 'the envoy must' (فرستاده subject, باید modal 'must/ought to'). یکی is an indefinite 'one / a single (person)' introducing the required quality. تیزویر is a poetic/compound adjective (marked in the lexicon as obscure) describing mental sharpness or keenness; here it functions as an attributive adjective to 'one'.
segments.s410-g48: سخن‌گوی و داننده و یادگیر is a coordinated list of required qualities: سخن‌گوی 'a speaker / eloquent one', داننده 'a knower / knowledgeable person', یادگیر 'a learner / quick at learning'. These nouns are predicative appositions to the 'one' introduced above, linked by و (and).
segments.s410-g49: Relative clause: 'سواری که باشد ورا فر و زیب' = 'a rider who has farr and beauty.' 'که' introduces the relative clause; 'باشد' is the classical/polite present/subjunctive form of 'to be' (here: 'has/is possessed of'). 'ورا' is an indirect-object marker meaning 'to him' (i.e., 'upon him' or 'he possesses'), and the nouns 'فر' and 'زیب' follow as the possessed qualities.
segments.s410-g50: 'نگیرد ورا رستم اندر فریب' has inverted word order: verb (negated present/subjunctive) + object + subject + prepositional phrase. 'نگیرد' (from گرفت#گیر) is active 'take/seize' used here in the sense 'take in/deceive' (negative: 'will not take in'). 'ورا' is the object 'him'; 'رستم' is the subject; 'اندر فریب' 'into/within deceit' indicates the manner (i.e. 'be taken in by deception').
segments.s410-g51: 'گر' introduces a conditional clause ('if'). 'ایدون' is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'one who comes' or simply 'comes'; 'که آید' repeats/clarifies with a relative 'who comes'. 'به نزدیک ما' is the directional phrase 'to/near us' (preposition + adjective + pronoun). The whole segment = 'If someone comes near us.'
segments.s410-g52: Main clause: 'درفشان کند رای تاریک ما.' 'درفشان' (the standard-bearer/banner-waver) is the agent; 'کند' is the classical present/subjunctive of 'to do/make' used optatively here ('let him do'). 'رای' = 'counsel/plan' modified by 'تاریک' ('dark' = secret/obscure), and 'ما' is the possessor 'our'. Word order: Subject Verb Object phrase, meaning 'let the standard-bearer execute/put into effect our secret counsel.'
segments.s410-g53: 'به خوبی دهد دست بند مرا' = 'He will, in kindness, give the bracelet to me.' 'به خوبی' is an adverbial phrase 'in goodness/kindness'; 'دهد' is the present/subjunctive 'he gives' (classical narrative mood); 'دست بند' is a compound noun 'wrist-band/bracelet' or 'fetter' (a bond placed on the hand), and 'مرا' marks the beneficiary/object 'me' (here split into tokens).
segments.s410-g54: 'به دانش ببندد گزند مرا' = 'With knowledge he will bind (prevent) harm to me.' 'به دانش' is instrumental ('by/with knowledge'); 'ببندد' (from بست#بند) is present/subjunctive 'he will bind/close' used transitively; 'گزند' = 'harm/injury' and 'مرا' is the patient 'me.' The idiom means he will, by wisdom, secure me against injury.
segments.s410-g55: 'نخواهم من او را به جز نیکویی' = 'I want/ask from him nothing but goodness.' 'نخواهم' is the first-person negative/present 'I do not want' or 'I will not wish'; 'من' is an emphatic subject pronoun; 'او را' is the accusative 'him'; 'به جز نیکویی' is the exception clause 'except goodness/kindness.'
segments.s410-g56: 'اگر دور دارد سر از بدخویی' is a conditional proviso: 'if he keeps (his) head far from ill-temper.' 'اگر' = 'if'; 'دور دارد' = 'keeps far' (literally 'has far'); 'سر' = 'head' used idiomatically for one's disposition/attitude; 'از بدخویی' = 'from ill-temper/badness.' Together: 'provided he remains free from ill temper.'

sentences.s410-s29: Peshotan said to him, “This is the way: abide by this and seek the honor of men.”
sentences.s410-s1: At the late-night hour when the cock crowed, the sound of the war-drum rose from the portal.
sentences.s410-s2: As, like an elephant, he set his foot onto the horse, the army sprang from their places like the wind.
sentences.s410-s3: He kept going until he came upon two paths; the elephant and the army remained standing in their place.
sentences.s410-s4: The fortress of Gonbadān had one road; another route, from the outset, ran a little toward the other side.
sentences.s410-s13: From there he went toward the Helmand, still fearful of being harmed.
sentences.s410-s14: As was customary they closed the pavilion; the great men of the army chose a place.
sentences.s410-s15: A ceremonial officer struck quickly and set up the throne; upon that throne the fortunate one mounted.
sentences.s410-s16: He brought (them) and summoned the peacemakers; he showered them with much gold and jewels.
sentences.s410-s5: The camel, behold, lay sleeping ahead; you said that he had been joined with the dust.
sentences.s410-s6: The caravan‑driver struck him on the head with a stick; because of the departure, the caravan remained then.
sentences.s410-s7: That came to the traveller as a bad omen; he commanded that they cut off his head and (take) his mane.
sentences.s410-s8: Know this: that when he returned to him there would be no harm, except the divine farr.
sentences.s410-s9: The attackers cut off his head; at that time his star returned to him.
sentences.s410-s10: From that Ashter a grief arose for Esfandiyar; at that time his ill‑omened, despised star seized him.
sentences.s410-s11: Thus it was said: whoever became victorious, the summit of his fortune became world‑illuminating.
sentences.s410-s12: Both good and bad were from God; a man's lips ought to be smiling.
sentences.s410-s17: He gladdened his own heart with repose and filled the hearts of the brave men with remembrance.
sentences.s410-s18: When the flower bloomed from the passing years, the faces of the renowned and the king faced (the) battle.
sentences.s410-s19: He said to his companions thus: that I did not swerve from the king's counsel, and I turned away from the road.
sentences.s410-s20: He told me much about Rostam's affair: 'Do not find any rest from chains and humiliation.'
sentences.s410-s21: I went to do it for my father; now this chosen, old, quarrelsome one...
sentences.s410-s22: He endures great hardship in place of the leaders of the world, and has set the world right with a heavy mace.
sentences.s410-s23: All the towns of Iran live by him; whether they are rulers or subjects.
sentences.s410-s24: The envoy must be a single sharp‑witted person — eloquent, knowledgeable, and quick to learn.
sentences.s410-s25: Rostam will not be deceived by a horseman who possesses farr and beauty.
sentences.s410-s26: If anyone comes near us, let the standard-bearer carry out our secret counsel.
sentences.s410-s27: He will kindly give me a bracelet; with learning he will ward off harm from me.
sentences.s410-s28: I ask nothing of him except goodness, provided he keeps his head far from ill-temper.
